Amrita H Nair
BackgroundBlogAbout

Background

Jun 25, 2021

I joined FOSS@Amrita, a club at Amrita University that promotes open source, in the freshman year of undergrad. Since then, I have actively participated in encouraging the club’s primary cause. Additionally, I guide my juniors at the club to the best of my abilities, a tradition started by the founders of the club(back in 2007) and continued by the incoming batches of students.

I started working on Ruby and Ruby on Rails in the first year of engineering and and at the end of second year, I was selected for the Rails Girls Summer of Code, a program which accepts 20 teams all over the world(2 students per team) each year and provides them with an open-source project to work on. It is a global fellowship program for women and non-binary coders.

As a part of this program, I had the opportunity to deliver a talk at the dotJS conference 2017 in Paris. My talk was on my work during the summer and a short description of the RGSoC initiative.

Hackathons are one of my favourite places to be in. The energy, the buzz and the chance to work with an extremely unique and driven set of people to create a prototype are what gets me excited. All through my undergraduate, I attended numerous hackathons, some within India while some international.

The first hackathon I attended was the APNIC Hackathon in Nepal. APNIC(Asia Pacific Network Information Center) is the Regional Internet Registry administering IP addresses for the Asia Pacific. The aim of APNIC 45 was to promote the spread of IPv6. Although it has been more than 20 years since the ‘new’ version of the Internet Protocol was introduced by the IETF, it has not been fully deployed yet. Different metrics show that many networks are already using IPv6 but still there is work to do.

Shout out to the 8 female participants of APNIC's first #Hackathon. They spent some time with Sylvia from @ApnicFoundation & @RIPE_NCC 's Vesna. Brilliant minds all of you! #womenintech #APRICOT2018 pic.twitter.com/8u6rThuAAQ

— APNIC (@apnic) February 25, 2018

A total of 25 hackers were chosen from the Asia-Pacific region. It was an honour to be selected to attend for this hackathon.

Around this time, I was awarded the WeTech Qualcomm Global Scholarship. This program is a partnership between the Institution of International Education and Qualcomm that invests in young women and future leaders in STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering and Math).

Through the four years of my undergraduate, I was also involved in programs that help guide women into tech. One of those initiatives was by the Rethink initiative. A program I participated in as a coach was the WIT Learning Program.

After completing my bachelors, I worked at Bang the Table, a startup focussed on building a product that focusses on community engagement. Bang the Table would later be acquired by Granicus. My role primiarly involved a lot of Rails and a little bit of dabbling with Kubernetes.

In fall of 2021, I will be be a part of the Erasmus-Mundus LCT program, completing the first year at Charles University, Prague and the second year at Saarland University, Germany. Wish me luck !
